# GateGuard System Design (IIT Gandhinagar)

We design a relational database for GateGuard with entities for **Person** (residents, students, visitors), **Vehicle**, **Gate**, **User/Role** (guards, admins), and **Access Logs**. The ER diagram below (Crow’s Foot notation per Elmasri & Navathe style) captures core entities and their relationships![][image1]  
. For example, one Person can have many entry/exit **Visits**; each visit is linked to a specific gate (entry and exit gate) and timestamp. Vehicles are similarly tracked via **VehicleVisit** records. Gates are referenced by all visits. Users (guards/admins) log into the system with assigned Roles. Each table uses primary keys (underlined) and foreign keys to enforce referential integrity. Attributes like Person.Name, Vehicle.LicensePlate, and Timestamp fields are NOT NULL to ensure data validity.

* **PersonType**(TypeID PK, TypeName): e.g. *Resident, Student, Visitor*. Normalizing person categories avoids repetition of type names[\[1\]](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/normalization-in-sql#:~:text=Normalization%20helps%20prevent%20data%20redundancy%2C,data%20manipulation%20within%20a%20database).

* **Person**(PersonID PK, Name, TypeID FK → PersonType, Department, Contact): stores individuals entering campus. The TypeID FK ensures valid categories (referential integrity).

* **VehicleType**(TypeID PK, TypeName): e.g. *PrivateCar, Taxi, Bike*.

* **Vehicle**(VehicleID PK, LicensePlate UNIQUE NOT NULL, TypeID FK → VehicleType, OwnerID FK → Person\[optional\]): stores vehicles. OwnerID references a Person (e.g. family car owner) but can be NULL for public taxis.

* **Gate**(GateID PK, Name, Location): campus gates (Gate 1, Gate 2, etc).

* **GateOccupancy**(GateID PK, OccupancyCount NOT NULL): tracks current occupancy at each gate. Triggers on entry/exit logs will update OccupancyCount in real time (increment on entry, decrement on exit). A check constraint (OccupancyCount \>= 0) ensures non-negative counts.

* **Role**(RoleID PK, RoleName): user roles (*Guard, Admin*).

* **User**(UserID PK, Username UNIQUE NOT NULL, PasswordHash NOT NULL, RoleID FK → Role): system users (guards/admins) with role-based access. We implement role-based access control – each user is assigned a predefined role with specific permissions[\[2\]](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/rbac#:~:text=What%20is%20role,RBAC). For example, a Guard role might allow logging entries but not altering user accounts.

* **PersonVisit**(VisitID PK, PersonID FK → Person NOT NULL, EntryGateID FK → Gate NOT NULL, EntryTime NOT NULL, ExitGateID FK → Gate, ExitTime, VehicleID FK → Vehicle): one record per person trip. A CHECK constraint enforces ExitTime \>= EntryTime (or ExitTime IS NULL if still inside) to ensure logical timing. This captures exactly **which gate** and **when** the person entered/exited, and optionally with which vehicle.

* **VehicleVisit**(VisitID PK, VehicleID FK → Vehicle NOT NULL, EntryGateID FK → Gate NOT NULL, EntryTime NOT NULL, ExitGateID FK → Gate, ExitTime): similarly logs each vehicle’s trip through a gate. A CHECK constraint ensures ExitTime \>= EntryTime.

This design uses at least 10 tables, each in 3NF or higher to eliminate redundancy[\[1\]](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/normalization-in-sql#:~:text=Normalization%20helps%20prevent%20data%20redundancy%2C,data%20manipulation%20within%20a%20database). For example, instead of repeating gate details in each visit, we reference GateID from the **Gate** table (upholding normalization and referential integrity). All foreign keys guarantee that, say, a PersonVisit.PersonID must exist in **Person**, preventing orphan records. Timestamps and IDs are NOT NULL where required, and unique constraints (like on LicensePlate) prevent duplicates.

![][image1]  
*Figure: Sample ERD (Chen’s Crow’s Foot notation) for an entry/exit system, showing entities (rectangles) and relationships (lines). Core entities Person, Vehicle, Gate, Role and their associations are illustrated (e.g., Person-Visit is 1-to-many, Visit includes EntryGate and ExitGate references).*

## UML Class Diagram

Below is a representative UML class diagram (inspired by a security system) that illustrates similar concepts![][image2]  
. In UML, **classes** (boxes) encapsulate attributes and operations, and relationships (associations, generalizations) show how they connect[\[3\]](https://www.visual-paradigm.com/guide/uml-unified-modeling-language/uml-class-diagram-tutorial/#:~:text=The%20UML%20Class%20diagram%20is,system%20by%20showing%20the%20system%27s)[\[4\]](https://www.cse.msu.edu/~cse870/Materials/main-tech-report-security-patterns.pdf#:~:text=The%20UML%20class%20diagram%20groups,oriented%20design). For GateGuard we would have classes like Person, Vehicle, Gate, Visit, User, Role, etc. For example, User might inherit subclasses Guard and Admin, or simply have a role attribute linked to a Role class.

class Person {  
  \+ PersonID: int  
  \+ Name: string  
  \+ Category: string  
}  
class Vehicle {  
  \+ VehicleID: int  
  \+ LicensePlate: string  
  \+ Type: string  
}  
class Gate {  
  \+ GateID: int  
  \+ Name: string  
}  
class Visit {  
  \+ VisitID: int  
  \+ EntryTime: datetime  
  \+ ExitTime: datetime  
  \+ EntryGateID: int  
  \+ ExitGateID: int  
}  
class Role {  
  \+ RoleID: int  
  \+ RoleName: string  
}  
class User {  
  \+ UserID: int  
  \+ Username: string  
  \+ PasswordHash: string  
  \+ RoleID: int  
}  
Person "1" \-- "0..\*" Visit : attends \>  
Vehicle "0..1" \-- "0..\*" Visit : carries \>  
Gate "1" \-- "0..\*" Visit : through \>  
User "1" \-- "1" Role : has \>

![][image2]  
*Figure: Example UML class diagram for an authentication system (roles, users, modules). In GateGuard, classes like User, Role, Gate, and Visit would appear similarly. UML class diagrams focus on static structure: classes with attributes and methods, and their relationships[\[3\]](https://www.visual-paradigm.com/guide/uml-unified-modeling-language/uml-class-diagram-tutorial/#:~:text=The%20UML%20Class%20diagram%20is,system%20by%20showing%20the%20system%27s).*

The UML view helps visualize that each class (e.g. Person, Gate, Visit) contains attributes (as columns in our tables) and possibly methods. The multiplicities (e.g. 1-to-many from Gate to Visit) match the ER relationships. UML and ER models are complementary: ER is used for the database schema, while UML describes the same entities as classes with operations (useful for software design).

## Core Functionalities and Relationships

* **Multi-gate Synchronization:** Each entry/exit record includes the specific GateID. The system can compute **real-time occupancy** per gate by aggregating logs (or via GateOccupancy). For example, current occupancy \= number of entries – exits for that gate. Triggers or background jobs can update GateOccupancy to keep it current.

* **Visitor/Resident Tracking:** A Person can have multiple PersonVisit records (one per in-out cycle). This supports tracking residents (professors), students, or guests. The PersonType table ensures we know who is a professor (resident) vs a visitor.

* **Vehicle/Person Sync:** If a student returns in a family car or taxi, we record both the PersonVisit (with VehicleID set to the car’s record) and the corresponding VehicleVisit. This links the person and vehicle in that transaction. A vehicle can enter without a person (e.g. delivery truck), which is still logged in VehicleVisit. Conversely, a person on foot can be logged with VehicleID=NULL.

* **Role-Based Access:** Guards and admins log in via the User table (with hashed passwords and RoleID). Guards have limited privileges (e.g. registering entries/exits), whereas Admins can manage users and view reports. This follows role-based access control (users get permissions via their role)[\[2\]](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/rbac#:~:text=What%20is%20role,RBAC).

All relationships use foreign keys so that, for example, a log record cannot reference a non-existent gate or person. We enforce business rules where possible: e.g., PersonVisit.ExitTime \>= PersonVisit.EntryTime. Normalization (e.g. separate Role table) prevents data anomalies and supports data integrity[\[1\]](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/normalization-in-sql#:~:text=Normalization%20helps%20prevent%20data%20redundancy%2C,data%20manipulation%20within%20a%20database).

## Suggested Indexing (B+ Trees)

To optimize query performance, we recommend B+ tree indexes on frequently queried columns. In particular, indexing **PersonID** and **Timestamp** in the visit logs will speed up lookups and range queries (e.g. “show all entries for Person X” or “all entries in date range”)[\[5\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/107132/what-columns-generally-make-good-indexes#:~:text=Indexes%20can%20play%20an%20important,select%20which%20columns%20to%20index). For example: \- CREATE INDEX idx\_person\_visit\_person ON PersonVisit(PersonID); \- CREATE INDEX idx\_person\_visit\_time ON PersonVisit(EntryTime, ExitTime); \- Similarly, index VehicleVisit(VehicleID) and its time columns.  
Since primary keys are automatically indexed (B-tree in InnoDB)[\[6\]](https://www.red-gate.com/simple-talk/databases/mysql/mysql-index-overviews-primary-key-indexes/#:~:text=Beneath%20the%20hood%2C%20,key%20relationships%2C%20clustering%2C%20and%20lookups), our PKs (VisitID, PersonID, etc.) will be clustered. Additional non-clustered B+tree indexes on foreign keys (GateID, UserID) and on the EntryTime/ExitTime columns will further accelerate joins and temporal queries[\[5\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/107132/what-columns-generally-make-good-indexes#:~:text=Indexes%20can%20play%20an%20important,select%20which%20columns%20to%20index)[\[6\]](https://www.red-gate.com/simple-talk/databases/mysql/mysql-index-overviews-primary-key-indexes/#:~:text=Beneath%20the%20hood%2C%20,key%20relationships%2C%20clustering%2C%20and%20lookups).

In summary, this design meets the multi-gate, multi-user requirements of a real IIT campus. It cleanly separates entities, enforces constraints (PK/FK, NOT NULL, logical checks), and anticipates performance needs with appropriate indexing[\[1\]](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/normalization-in-sql#:~:text=Normalization%20helps%20prevent%20data%20redundancy%2C,data%20manipulation%20within%20a%20database)[\[5\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/107132/what-columns-generally-make-good-indexes#:~:text=Indexes%20can%20play%20an%20important,select%20which%20columns%20to%20index).

**Sources:** Database normalization and design best practices[\[1\]](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/normalization-in-sql#:~:text=Normalization%20helps%20prevent%20data%20redundancy%2C,data%20manipulation%20within%20a%20database); UML class diagram concepts[\[3\]](https://www.visual-paradigm.com/guide/uml-unified-modeling-language/uml-class-diagram-tutorial/#:~:text=The%20UML%20Class%20diagram%20is,system%20by%20showing%20the%20system%27s)[\[4\]](https://www.cse.msu.edu/~cse870/Materials/main-tech-report-security-patterns.pdf#:~:text=The%20UML%20class%20diagram%20groups,oriented%20design); role-based access control (RBAC) fundamentals[\[2\]](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/rbac#:~:text=What%20is%20role,RBAC); B-tree indexing and query optimization[\[6\]](https://www.red-gate.com/simple-talk/databases/mysql/mysql-index-overviews-primary-key-indexes/#:~:text=Beneath%20the%20hood%2C%20,key%20relationships%2C%20clustering%2C%20and%20lookups)[\[5\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/107132/what-columns-generally-make-good-indexes#:~:text=Indexes%20can%20play%20an%20important,select%20which%20columns%20to%20index). The ER and UML diagrams use standard notation as in Elmasri & Navathe.
